Here is a concise overview of what the **Figma** plugin adds and how to use it.

## What you get

**MCP server (`figma`)**  
Runs the official Figma MCP so the agent can:

- **Read from Figma**: design context, screenshots, metadata; FigJam via the right flows.
- **Write into Figma**: create or change nodes, layouts, variables, components, etc. (mainly through scripted Figma/plugin-style actions).
- **FigJam diagrams**: e.g. `generate_diagram` for flowcharts, sequence diagrams, ERDs, timelines, etc., when that workflow is enabled.
- **Code Connect**: map Figma components to code snippets and work with those mappings.

**Skills (load these before certain tool calls — the agent should do this automatically)**  

| Skill | Role |
|--------|------|
| **figma-use** | Required before **`use_figma`** (create/edit/read that needs code running in the file). |
| **figma-create-new-file** | Required before **`create_new_file`** for a new Figma / FigJam / Slides file. |
| **figma-generate-diagram** | Required before **`generate_diagram`**. |
| **figma-generate-design** | Full pages / modals / panels from code or descriptions; use with **figma-use**. |
| **figma-generate-library** | Design systems from code (tokens, components, theming). |
| **figma-code-connect** | `.figma.ts` / `.figma.js` Code Connect templates. |
| **figma-use-figjam** / **figma-use-slides** | Extra context for FigJam and Slides with **`use_figma`**. |

**Slash commands**  
You can trigger the same workflows with **`/`** (e.g. **`/figma-create-new-file figjam My board`**) so the right skill runs before the tool.

**Rules & hooks**  
Anything the plugin registers as project rules or hooks applies on its own; you do not need to repeat those in chat.

## Authentication

There is **no `STATUS.md`** in this workspace’s Figma MCP folder (`mcps/plugin-figma-figma` only has things like `INSTRUCTIONS.md` and tool descriptors). Figma’s MCP still usually needs you to be **signed into Figma and connected from Cursor** (per Cursor’s MCP / Figma integration UI). If tools error or ask for auth, open **Cursor Settings → MCP / Figma** (or the Figma connection flow your Cursor build shows) and complete sign-in there.

**Summary:** The plugin wires **official Figma MCP tools** plus **skills** that gate **`use_figma`**, **`create_new_file`**, and **`generate_diagram`**, and supports **Code Connect** and **design-system / canvas** workflows. Use **`/`** for explicit skill entry points; rely on Cursor + Figma auth if reads/writes fail.

Here is what Figma node **[1:67 “Items”](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-67&m=dev)** describes and how the code was brought in line.

## Figma vs code

The frame is the **two-column masonry grid** (165pt-wide cards, 20pt gutter, left column offset **31pt**). That layout was already correct in `app/index.tsx` (`colW`, `marginTop: 31 * scale`, `LEFT_PRODUCTS` / `RIGHT_PRODUCTS` order and `cardHeight`s). The main drift was in **`ProductCard`**.

## Changes in `ProductCard.tsx`

1. **Typography** — Figma stack uses **letter-spacing -0.3** on category, title, and price (`tracking-[-0.3px]`). Category and price were at `-0.2`; they’re now **`-0.3`**. Added **`lineHeight`** (18 / 22 / 18) and **4pt** spacing between lines so the block reads like the design.

2. **Text band** — Removed the extra **8pt** top padding on the copy block so the label stack sits where the design expects after the image band. The lower area uses a **fixed height `L.textZone * s`** (84 / 97pt scaled) with **32pt** bottom padding so the three lines + air match the **97pt** text zone on the tall left cards.

3. **Ground shadow (bikes only)** — In the file, **Road Bike** and **Mountain Bike** cards use the small **shadow** asset under the product; **Road Helmet** cards do not. Added a scaled **ellipse strip** (`114×6` left column, **`144×6`** right column, **x = 30 / 10** from the card edge) so it tracks nodes **1:71 / 1:85 / 1:127** without relying on expiring MCP asset URLs.

4. **Heart** — Bikes use the **accent gradient** heart; helmets use **white**, matching the mix of treatments in that grid (and the `HeartOutlineIcon` variants).

`CardBackground` was already aligned with Rectangle **166** geometry and gradients; no change there.

## Doc

`app/index.tsx` header comment now links both **1:38** (screen) and **1:67** (Items grid).

---

If anything still feels off on a device, the next knobs are **shadow opacity** / **vertical offset** (`bottom: 10 * s`) and **text `paddingBottom`** inside the fixed `textZone` height. Say if you want those tuned to a screenshot pixel-match.

Here’s what changed.

### New: `components/DiscoverProductGrid.tsx`

This is the **items / masonry grid** in one place. You can change layout, data, or styling there without touching the rest of Discover.

**Props**

| Prop | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `bandWidth` | Same as hero `cardW` so columns match `ProductCard` math. |
| `onProductPress` | Called when a card is pressed. |
| `leftProducts` / `rightProducts` | Optional — default `LEFT_PRODUCTS` / `RIGHT_PRODUCTS` from `constants/products.ts`. |
| `leftColumnTopOffset` | Design pt @ 390 (default `31`, Figma left column drop). |
| `columnGap` | Gutter in design pt (default `20`). |
| `style` | Extra styles on the outer row `View`. |

### Updated: `app/index.tsx`

Replaced the inline grid with:

```tsx
<DiscoverProductGrid
  bandWidth={cardW}
  onProductPress={handleProductPress}
/>
```

Imports for `ProductCard`, `LEFT_PRODUCTS`, and `RIGHT_PRODUCTS` were removed from the screen; grid-only styles (`grid`, `column`) live on the new component.

**Customize examples**

```tsx

  bandWidth={cardW}
  onProductPress={handleProductPress}
  leftProducts={myLeft}
  rightProducts={myRight}
  leftColumnTopOffset={24}
```

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
@components/DiscoverProductGrid.tsx where in this code can i see where the placement relative to the phone screen is determined
</user_query>

**You mostly don’t.** `DiscoverProductGrid.tsx` only decides **layout inside its own row** (two columns, gutter, left column dropped a bit). It does **not** choose where that row sits on the phone (left/right/center vs screen, or how far down the page it is).

### What *is* tied to the phone here

- **Lines 37–38** — `useWindowDimensions()` and `scale = width / 390` turn design “pt” into something that scales with **screen width**.
- **Lines 39–40** — `colGap` and `colW` from `bandWidth` set **how wide each column is** (still only *inside* the grid, not on-screen X/Y).
- **Lines 43–48** — The only “placement” here is **relative to the grid itself**: left column gets `marginTop: leftColumnTopOffset * scale` so it sits lower than the right column (Figma stagger). The right column has no extra top margin (lines 61–62).

So: **horizontal position on the phone** and **vertical position in the scroll** are **not** in this file.

### Where screen placement actually comes from

In **`app/index.tsx`**, the grid sits inside a centered content band:

- `ScrollView` with `contentContainerStyle={{ alignItems: 'center', width, ... }}` centers children horizontally.
- Inner `View style={{ width: cardW }}` sets the band width; **`DiscoverProductGrid` gets `bandWidth={cardW}`**, so the grid is exactly that wide and **centered** because of the parent.

Anything above/below the grid (hero, categories) is also order and spacing in that same `ScrollView` — that’s what fixes the grid **vertically** in the page flow.

**Short answer:** In `DiscoverProductGrid.tsx` you only see **intra-grid** placement (`marginTop` on the left column, `gap`, column widths). **On-screen** placement is determined by **`app/index.tsx`** (ScrollView + centered `width: cardW` wrapper), not this component.

<user_query>
the items grid is too close to the left of the screen, what code determines the available spacing on the left and right
</user_query>

The grid doesn’t set screen insets itself. **Horizontal space comes from the Discover screen:** how wide the content band is (`cardW`) and the fact that band is **centered** in the scroll area.

### 1. How wide the band is (including grid)

```68:70:c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome\app\index.tsx
  const scale = width / 390;
  // Content band width (Figma 350pt @ 390); symmetric margins: (width − cardW) / 2 each side
  const cardW = Math.min(350 * scale, width - 32);
```

- **`350 * scale`** — design width at 390pt baseline (`scale = width / 390`).
- **`width - 32`** — on **narrow** screens this wins: the band is at most the screen minus **32** total horizontal px → about **16 px per side** minimum before centering.

So **`32`** (and the **`350`** Figma width) are what cap how wide the column can get and therefore how much margin is left.

### 2. Why left and right match

```143:155:c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome\app\index.tsx
      <ScrollView
        style={styles.scroll}
        contentContainerStyle={{
          paddingTop: lx.padTop,
          paddingBottom: 8,
          width,
          alignItems: 'center',
        }}
        ...
      >
        {/* Full-width band centers the 350pt-scaled column so L/R margins match */}
        <View style={{ width: cardW, paddingHorizontal: 0 }}>
```

- **`width`** on the scroll content = full window width.
- **`alignItems: 'center'`** centers the child **`View`** whose width is **`cardW`**.

So **each side’s margin** is:

\[
\frac{\text{width} - \text{cardW}}{2}
\]

### 3. How the grid gets that width

`DiscoverProductGrid` is given `bandWidth={cardW}` from the same file — it only fills that centered band.

---

**If it feels too close to the left:** widen the inset by changing **`width - 32`** (e.g. `width - 48` for ~24px per side when the cap applies), or lower the design band (e.g. use **`340 * scale`** instead of `350`), or add **`paddingHorizontal`** on the `ScrollView`’s `contentContainerStyle` so everything (header, hero, grid) shifts inward together.

content.text:
Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
`ScrollView`’s `contentContainerStyle` used `alignItems: 'center'` with a single child whose width was `cardW`. On some setups the scroll **content width shrink-wraps** to that child, so the “center” is computed in a **narrow** strip and the band **sticks to the left** of the screen while the right side looks empty — i.e. **asymmetric** even though the math for `cardW` was symmetric.

### Fix (in `app/index.tsx`)
1. **`minWidth: width`** and **`alignItems: 'stretch'`** on the scroll content so the row is at least full screen wide before centering.
2. A **full-width wrapper** with explicit `width` and **`paddingHorizontal: layoutPadH`** where  
   `layoutPadH = Math.max(insets.left, insets.right)`  
   so safe-area cutouts use the **larger** inset on **both** sides when they differ.
3. **`cardW`** is now based on the space **inside** that padding:  
   `innerWidth = width - 2 * layoutPadH`  
   `cardW = Math.min(350 * scale, innerWidth - 32)`  
   so the band stays centered in the padded area with the same 16pt-style clearance on each side.

### `ProductCard` / grid
`ProductCard` used to infer column width from `width - 32` while the screen passed a different `cardW`. It now takes a required **`bandWidth`** prop (from `DiscoverProductGrid`) so **grid and card scaling always match** after the new `cardW` rule.

You should see **equal left/right spacing** for the header, hero, categories, and grid. If you want more air on both sides, increase the **`32`** in `innerWidth - 32` (e.g. `40` or `48`).

content.text:
Here’s what that Metro log means and what we changed.

### What the warning is
On **iOS and Android**, `react-native-svg` does **not** implement most SVG filter primitives (`FeFlood`, `FeGaussianBlur`, `FeComposite`, `FeMerge`, etc.). Anything using `<Filter>`… was effectively a no-op on native and triggered the long **“Some of the used filters are not yet supported”** warning.

### What we changed
All `<Filter>` / `Fe*` usage was removed and replaced with **React Native box shadows** on the wrapping `View`s (which *are* supported):

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| **`BottomTabBar.tsx`** | Pill: drop shadow filter removed; pill `Svg` wrapped in a `View` with `shadowColor` / `shadowOffset` / `shadowOpacity` / `shadowRadius` (Figma-ish dy≈20, blur≈15, `#10141c` @ 60%) + `elevation` on Android. `pillTrack` uses `overflow: 'visible'` so the shadow isn’t clipped. |
| **`CardBackground.tsx`** | Card shape: filter removed; same kind of shadow on the outer `View` (primary “below” shadow only — RN can’t stack two independent shadows like the old dual Fe* filter). |
| **`HeroBackground.tsx`** | Hero shape: same pattern — filter removed, `View` shadow + `elevation`. |

After a refresh, that **Fe\*** filter warning should be gone (unless another file adds filters later).

**Trade-off:** Shadows are now **bounding-box** style (iOS/Android), not the exact SVG filter shape, and the **second** “glow above” shadow from Figma isn’t duplicated on cards/hero—only a single downward shadow is approximated. If you want the upper highlight back, we’d add a second subtle layer (e.g. a very light semi-transparent duplicate path or a thin gradient overlay), still without SVG filters.

content.text:
Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
`ProductCard`’s `CardBackground` sits **`40 * s` px to the left** of each column (`PAD_X`, scaled by `s = colW / 165`). The column is flush with the **left edge of the content band**, so that bleed sits in the **screen margin**. On a **390px-wide** band, `(390 − 350) / 2 = 20px` margin per side, but bleed is **~40px** — so the card **draws past** the intended margin and hugs the left edge. The right column bleeds toward the **gutter**, so the layout **looks** uneven even when the band is centered.

### Fix (in `app/index.tsx`)
`cardW` is now capped with a **closed-form max width** so the margin is at least the left bleed:

- `colGap = 20 * scale`
- `cardW ≤ (165 * innerWidth + 40 * colGap) / 205`  
  (same as `(innerWidth + (8/33) * colGap) * 33/41` — derived from  
  `(innerWidth − cardW) / 2 ≥ 40 * (cardW − colGap) / 330`)
- Still `≤ 350 * scale` (Figma band) and `≤ innerWidth − 32` (minimum clearance)

On a **390pt** screen this caps the band at **~318px**, so categories, hero, and grid stay **aligned** but sit farther in, with **enough** side space for the card shadow/parallelogram bleed. On **wider** phones the cap often stays at **350×scale** because the margin is already large enough.

No updates to `DiscoverProductGrid` or `ProductCard` are required: they already use `bandWidth={cardW}`.

If you want **more** breathing room after this, raise the `innerWidth - 32` floor (e.g. to `48`).

Here is a structured review of **why the Discover “hero + categories + grid” block can look horizontally wrong**, why several fixes only helped partially, and what is still in play.

---

## 1. What “correct” even means here

There are **three different coordinate systems** mixed together:

| Layer | What sets horizontal position |
|--------|--------------------------------|
| **Screen** | `useWindowDimensions().width`, optional safe-area |
| **Content band** | Centered strip of width `cardW` inside the scroll area |
| **Each card column** | Width `colW = (bandWidth − colGap) / 2`, with **SVG + bleed drawn outside that width** |

A layout can be **mathematically centered** while still **looking** left-heavy, because the eye follows **ink** (gradient, parallelogram, shadows), not flex boxes.

---

## 2. Root cause A — Geometry outside the layout box (`CardBackground`)

`ProductCard` does **not** draw the card only inside `colW`. `CardBackground` is absolutely positioned with **`left: -PAD_X * s`** (`PAD_X = 40` design units), and width **`245 * s`** (165pt shape + 40pt bleed each side in design space).

So relative to the **column** (which is already flush to the **left edge of `cardW`** for the left column):

- The **layout** column starts at the band’s left edge.
- The **painted** shape starts **~`40 * s` px to the left** of that edge.

That bleed is **by design** (Figma export includes shadow bleed in the SVG canvas). It is **not** a bug in flex — it is **extra pixels that eat into the margin between the band and the physical screen**.

So if the screen margin is **M** and geometric bleed is **B ≈ 40·s** (with `s = colW / 165`):

- You need **M ≥ B** (roughly) or the card will visually cross into the “margin” and hug the screen.
- When **M < B**, the problem reads as **“grid too close on the left”** even if `cardW` is centered.

**Why early fixes “failed”:**  
Increasing `alignItems: 'center'` / `minWidth` fixes **centering of `cardW`**, but does **not** remove **leftward paint** unless **`cardW` shrinks** or **bleed shrinks**. So centering alone could not fix the symptom.

**Later fix (bleed cap):**  
`cardW ≤ (165·innerWidth + 40·colGap) / 205` enforces **M ≥ B** for the **geometric** `-40·s` offset. That addresses the **dominant** structural issue.

---

## 3. Root cause B — RN shadow extends past the SVG bleed box

`CardBackground`’s wrapper `View` now uses **`shadowOffset` / `shadowRadius` / `elevation`** (SVG filters were removed for native). Those shadows are applied to the **same** outer box that already extends **`PAD_X * s`** left of the column.

On iOS especially, **`shadowRadius` spreads in all directions** around the view’s bounds. So the **first visible dark pixels** can sit **left of** `−PAD_X·s` by something on the order of **a fraction of `shadowRadius` (here `15 * s`)**.

Your closed-form cap uses **40** from `PAD_X` in the numerator, **not** `40 + shadowMargin`. So:

- The math can still be **slightly optimistic**.
- Someone sensitive to shadow or on a device that draws a larger halo can still say “it’s too tight on the left” after the formula.

**Why this feels like “fixes keep failing”:**  
Each fix addressed one layer (scroll centering, then bleed), but **stacked outward effects** (bleed + shadow halo + optional elevation) are easy to **under-account** in one formula.

---

## 4. Root cause C — Parallelogram is not symmetric left vs right

The path is a **rounded parallelogram**: the **right** vertical edge sits higher than the **left** (Figma notes in `CardBackground.tsx`). So within the same `colW`:

- The **opaque fill** is not a centered rectangle; mass shifts with the slant.
- The **left** column’s card and the **right** column’s card relate differently to the **gutter** and to the **outer screen margin** (right column’s bleed goes more into the **center gap**; left column’s bleed goes into the **outer** margin).

So even with **equal** `M` left and right of the **band**, you can still get **“more air on the right”** because **visual weight** is not symmetric. That reads as a spacing bug but is partly **shape + composition**, not wrong `marginLeft`.

---

## 5. Root cause D — Full-screen diagonal accent (`Svg` polygon)

The blue polygon uses points like **negative x** (`-20 * scale`) and spans the **full `width`**. It is **not** clipped to `cardW`.

So the **background** is intentionally asymmetric: strong blue on one side, dark navy on the other. The **product band** can be perfectly centered while the **background gradient** still makes one side feel “busier” or “emptier.” That can **reinforce** a feeling that the grid is shifted, even when the grid isn’t.

---

## 6. Root cause E — ScrollView + centering quirks (why the first fixes were necessary)

Originally, `ScrollView` + `alignItems: 'center'` + a child whose width was `cardW` sometimes led to **content width shrink-wrapping** or ambiguous cross-axis behavior. The child was not reliably laid out in a **full-width** flex region, so the band could **stick left** while the right side of the screen showed **unused** space — **true** layout asymmetry.

**Fix:** `minWidth: width`, `alignItems: 'stretch'`, and an explicit **`width` + `paddingHorizontal: layoutPadH`** wrapper. That targets **real** centering bugs, not bleed.

If someone still runs an **old bundle** or edits **`app\index.tsx` vs `app/index.tsx`** duplicate on Windows, they might not see this layer at all — worth confirming a single source of truth in git.

---

## 7. Root cause F — Small but real: category strip padding

Horizontal `ScrollView` uses **`paddingRight: lx.catPr`** but **no `paddingLeft`** in `contentContainerStyle`. That does **not** move the first pill off the left edge of the band, but it does make **scroll content** slightly asymmetric. It’s minor compared to A–D but worth knowing for pixel-polish.

---

## 8. What actually interacts (summary)

```
Screen edge
    ←—— margin M ——→
                    [ cardW band, centered ]
                    ↑ left column col 0
                         CardBackground: layout at -40·s from column
                         + RN shadow halo beyond that
                         + slanted fill (asymmetric “mass”)
    Background polygon (full width, asymmetric) under everything
```

**Failures were “partial”** because different attempts hit **different terms** in that sum: centering fixed **M’s distribution**; bleed cap fixed **`B` vs `M`**; nothing in JS removes **parallelogram asymmetry** or **full-screen background composition** or **full shadow halo** without further shrinking or clipping.

---

## 9. What would make this robust (if it still bothers you)

1. **Padding budget for shadow**, not only `PAD_X`: e.g. require  
   `M ≥ 40·s + c·shadowRadius` (pick `c` empirically ~0.5–1 on iOS).  
2. **Clip or reduce bleed on edge columns** (e.g. smaller `PAD_X` when `column === 'left'` near screen — invasive).  
3. **Accept** a slightly narrower `cardW` than Figma’s 350 @ 390 on narrow phones (you already do when the bleed cap binds).  
4. **Optional inner gutter** inside `cardW` for grid + categories only (would need hero width decision).  
5. **Confirm one `index.tsx`** and a clean Metro reload so you’re not chasing a stale layout.

---

## 10. Short verdict

- **Primary quantitative cause:** **`CardBackground` draws significantly left of the column** (`-PAD_X * s`), so **margin must be large enough** or the left edge always looks crowded.  
- **Why multiple fixes seemed to fail:** Early work fixed **centering**; the dominant issue was **out-of-layout painting** + **shadow halo**, which centering alone cannot fix.  
- **Why it can still feel asymmetric after the cap:** **Shadow spread**, **slant**, and **full-screen background** are still **asymmetric by design**, so “perfectly equal whitespace” to the **visible** card silhouette is not the same as “equal `cardW` margins.”

If you want, the next concrete step is to add a **small safety term** (e.g. `+ 12 * scale` or `+ 0.5 * shadowRadius`) into the margin-vs-bleed inequality and tune once on a device screenshot.

Here is what was finished for the **1:38 layout** and **1:67-aligned** Discover band.

### Layout (Figma 1:38 — 20 + 350 + 20)

In `app/index.tsx` the content width now follows the frame math: **20pt** side margins and a **350pt** band (scaled), capped only by the usable inner width after safe-area padding:

```73:75:c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome\app\index.tsx
  // Figma Discover 1:38 — content strip: 20pt margin + 350pt band + 20pt (frame 390 wide).
  const marginH = 20 * scale;
  const cardW = Math.min(350 * scale, innerWidth - 2 * marginH);
```

**Cause:** In `CardBackground`, the SVG is centered in the column, but the path only uses **20pt** of the left viewBox padding and **60pt** on the right (`viewBox` 245pt, path x = 20…185). `transform: [{ translateX: 20 * s }]`  
so the pair of columns lines up with the 350pt band the way Figma intends.

## Review: alignment inside `ProductCard`

### What was already solid
- **Column split:** Left vs right use different `imgPadH`, `imgPadTop`, `textPadL/R`, `heartTop`, and `textZone` per the comments (Figma Items 1 vs 3).
- **Vertical split:** `imgAreaH = cardH − SLANT·s − textZone·s` keeps the image band and text band proportional to each card’s `cardHeight`.
- **Heart:** 24×24 scaled by `s`, offsets scaled — consistent with the rest of the card.
- **Ground shadow:** Different `shadowLeft` / `shadowW` per column matches the asymmetry called out in code.

### Issues found (and addressed)
1. **Typography didn’t scale with `s`** — Paddings, icon, and slant all use `s = colW / 165`, but `StyleSheet` fixed **13 / 15 / 13** and **18 / 22 / 18** line heights. On widths ≠ 390pt baseline, type stayed “screen px” while the card grew/shrank in **design space**, so text looked too large/small vs Figma and vs horizontal padding.
2. **Text block bottom padding** — Fixed `32 * s` for every card; the three-line stack is **66** design-pt tall (`18 + 4 + 22 + 4 + 18`). For `textZone` **97** / **84**, the remainder should be **`(textZone − 66) * s`** so the block fills the Figma text frame without drifting.
3. **Long titles** — `numberOfLines={1}` now has **`ellipsizeMode="tail"`** so truncation matches typical product-row behavior.

### What we did not change (optional later)
- **Parallelogram vs box:** Content is laid in a **rectangular** strip under the slant; the visible shape is skewed. Matching every pixel to the inner “safe” polygon would need extra horizontal inset by **y** (heavier change). For this pass we stayed with the existing Figma-driven box model.
- **`CardBackground`’s `col` prop** — Still unused for mirroring; comment says both columns share the same path; OK to leave as-is.

### Code
- `TEXT` constants + `useMemo` → `textStyle` with scaled `fontSize` / `lineHeight` / `marginTop` and **`padBottom`** from `textZone`.
- `StyleSheet` text entries only keep **font**, **color**, **letterSpacing**.

`tsc --noEmit` passes on the updated file.

content.text:
## What’s going wrong

Expo itself is almost certainly fine. What broke is **starting the dev server**, because **port 8081 is already taken**.

From the run we did:

```text
› Port 8081 is being used by another process
Input is required, but 'npx expo' is in non-interactive mode.
Required input:
> Use port 8082 instead?
› Skipping dev server
```

So Metro never came up: it wanted to ask “use 8082?” but couldn’t (non-interactive / no TTY), and **bailed out**.

On your machine, **8081 is held by `node.exe` (PID 2872)** — very often an **old Metro/Expo** that stayed running after you closed a terminal or stopped the UI without stopping the server.

So it “was fine a little while ago” because nothing was on 8081; then a previous session left **Node still listening on 8081**.

---

## What to do

1. **Stop the old server**  
   - Find the terminal/Cursor tab where Expo is still running and stop it (**Ctrl+C**), **or**  
   - Task Manager → end **Node** that’s using 8081, **or** (PowerShell, only if you’re sure it’s the stray Metro):
   ```powershell
   Stop-Process -Id 2872
   ```
   (PID may change next time; `netstat -ano | findstr :8081` to find the current one.)

2. **Or avoid 8081**  
   ```powershell
   npx expo start --port 8082
   ```

3. **Cursor / automation**  
   `--non-interactive` isn’t supported; Expo suggests **`CI=1`** for non-interactive runs — but with a port conflict, **CI mode still can’t answer the port prompt**, so you either free 8081 or pass **`--port`** explicitly.

---

## Not a code-review regression

Nothing in the recent React/layout edits would stop Expo from starting; this is **environment / process / port** behavior. After freeing 8081 or using `--port 8082`, `npx expo start` should behave like before.

Here’s what was going on and how to run Expo reliably.

## What’s wrong

1. **Port 8081 is already in use** (another `node` / Metro / Expo, often PID like **20304**). Default `npx expo start` tries **8081** first; if it can’t bind and you’re not in an interactive terminal, it may **exit without a clear “success”** or keep failing.
2. **The project itself is fine** — `npx expo-doctor` reported **17/17 checks passed**, and starting with **`--port 8083`** brought up Metro: `Waiting on http://localhost:8083`.

## What to do (pick one)

**A — Avoid 8081 (simplest)**  
From the project folder:

```powershell
npm run start:8083
```

That script is now in your `package.json`. Scan the QR from that terminal; the URL will use **8083**.

**B — Reclaim 8081**  
Find the process:

```powershell
netstat -ano | findstr :8081
```

Then end the PID shown in the last column (only if it’s an old dev server):

```powershell
taskkill /PID 20304 /F
```

(Replace **20304** with whatever PID you see.) After that, `npm start` / `npx expo start` on **8081** should work again.

**C — Phone can’t load**  
Same Wi‑Fi as the PC, correct port in the URL, and Windows Firewall allowing Node for **private** networks.

---

A stray Metro instance that was started here on **8083** has been stopped so it won’t block your next run. Use **`npm run start:8083`** whenever **8081** is busy.

It then ended with **exit code 1** and no error in the log — that usually means the process was **stopped from outside** (for example Metro on that port was **force-killed** so the parent `expo` process exited with a failure code), not that your app failed to bundle.

**Follow-up:** Nothing you need to fix for that. To run Expo yourself, use a normal terminal and leave it open, e.g. `npm run start:8083` or `npx expo start --port 8083`.

content.text:
That message almost always means **the device can’t download the JS bundle** (or Expo Go’s own compatibility payload), not that your repo is broken. Your app doesn’t use `expo-updates`; this is almost always **Expo Go ↔ dev server / network**.

### 1. **Expo Go must match your SDK (you’re on SDK 52)**  
Install the **latest Expo Go** from the [Play Store](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=host.exp.exponent) / [App Store](https://apps.apple.com/app/expo-go/id982107779). Old Expo Go often can’t load newer projects and surfaces errors like this.

### 2. **Phone can’t reach your PC (most common on LAN)**  
- PC and phone on the **same Wi‑Fi** (not guest / isolated AP).  
- In the Expo terminal, open with **`exp://YOUR_LAN_IP:PORT`** — not `localhost` (the phone isn’t your PC).  
- **Windows:** set Wi‑Fi to **Private**; allow **Node.js** through the firewall for **Private** networks.

### 3. **Try tunnel mode** (gets around LAN/firewall issues)  
```powershell
cd "c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome"
npx expo start --tunnel
```  
First run may ask to install `@expo/ngrok` — accept. Then scan the new QR.

### 4. **VPN / corporate network**  
Turn VPN off or try **phone hotspot** + PC on that hotspot, then `expo start` again.

### 5. **Clear bad state**  
Force-close Expo Go, clear its cache if the app offers it, or **reinstall Expo Go**.

---

**Quick order to try:** update Expo Go → `npx expo start --tunnel` → if still failing, same Wi‑Fi + firewall + no VPN. If you say whether you’re on **Android or iOS** and **LAN vs tunnel**, we can narrow it one step further.

content.text:
Here’s what was going on and what we changed.

### Cause
`CardBackground` draws the shape in a **245pt-wide** viewBox, but the path only uses **x = 20 → 185** (165pt). That’s **20pt** tighter on the left of the path than on the right inside the viewBox. The SVG is still **centered in the column** with `left: -40·s`, so the **visible card** sits a bit **to the left** of the column’s math center.

All **content** (image, text, heart) was laid out in the full column (`left: 0` … `right: 0`), i.e. centered in **that** box. So it looked **shifted right** compared to the real shape—like things were sticking out past the card on the right.

### Fix
In `ProductCard.tsx`:

1. **`CONTENT_SHIFT_X = 20`** (design units, same scale as `s`) — matches that horizontal offset.
2. The **absolute content** wrapper (heart + image + text) now uses  
   **`transform: [{ translateX: -CONTENT_SHIFT_X * s }]`** so it lines up with the parallelogram.
3. **`overflow: 'visible'`** on the animated root, `TouchableOpacity`, and that wrapper so the shift doesn’t get clipped.

Hearts, product images, and the text block all move together, so they stay aligned with each other and with the card chrome.

If anything still looks a pixel or two off on a specific device, we can tune `CONTENT_SHIFT_X` slightly (e.g. 18–22).

content.text:
Here’s what was added and how it behaves.

### Approach
- **iOS / Android:** `@react-native-masked-view/masked-view` masks an **`expo-blur` `BlurView`** to the same hero path as in Figma, so the **content behind the card is blurred** inside the shape (diffused / glassy), not shown sharply through a transparent gradient.
- On top of the blur, the **R166 gradient** is drawn at **`FIGMA_HERO.overlayFillOpacity`** (~0.52) so it still reads as the navy sheet from the file.
- The **hairline border** stays a separate SVG on top (same as before).
- **Android:** `experimentalBlurMethod="dimezisBlurView"` is set so blur is actually applied (default is effectively no blur). `blurReductionFactor` is set to **3.2** so strength is closer to iOS.
- **Web:** Still a **single SVG** fill at **`fillOpacityFlat`** — masked native blur isn’t dependable on web.

### Dependencies
- **`@react-native-masked-view/masked-view`** — installed with `expo install` (already in `package.json`).

### Tokens (`constants/theme.ts` → `FIGMA_HERO`)
- **`blurIntensity`** — default **52** (blur strength).
- **`overlayFillOpacity`** — default **0.52** (tint over blur; lower = more “frost”, higher = more solid).
- **`fillOpacityFlat`** — **0.92** for the web-only path.

Tune **`blurIntensity`** and **`overlayFillOpacity`** together until it matches your Figma frame.

### Other fix
`ProductCard` was not passing **`fillStyle`** into **`CardBackground`** (so `cardFillStyle` was unused and column fills could be wrong). That’s wired again with **`fillStyle={cardFillStyle}`**.

content.text:
Here’s what was wrong and what we changed.

### 1. White borders
`CategoryPill` always used `borderWidth: 1` with light `borderColor` on both **active** (accent) and **inactive** (R166) pills. That’s removed: **`pillBase` no longer sets a stroke** — depth comes from the existing **upper glow** (`glowWrap`) and **lower shadow** (`dropBelow`) only, like your reference.

### 2. Last two pills = frosted, not solid
Mountain and Accessory sit over the **cyan** shape, so they should read as **glass**, not the same opaque R166 as Electric/Road.

- **`app/index.tsx`** — passes **`frosted={i >= CATEGORIES.length - 2}`** so the **last two** entries (`Mountain`, `Accessory`) get the glass treatment.
- **`CategoryPill.tsx`** — for **`frosted && !isActive`**:
  - **iOS / Android:** `MaskedView` (rounded 50×50 mask) + **`BlurView`** + a **semi-transparent R167-style** `LinearGradient` on top (`GLASS_OVERLAY`), same idea as the hero card but tuned for a small control (`intensity` 34, Android `dimezisBlurView`).
  - **Web:** no masked blur — **`GLASS_OVERLAY_WEB`** gradient only so it still looks softer and more transparent than the solid pills.
- Icons on frosted inactive pills use **`COLORS.white`** so they stay clear on the bright background.

Active pill (e.g. “All” or any selected category) is still the **full accent gradient**, with **no border**, unchanged aside from dropping the stroke.

Here’s what was wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
`CheckoutSlider` used **`TRACK_WIDTH = Math.min(width - 40, 350)`**, so on a **390pt-wide** frame the track was **350px** — basically the full content width. Figma **1:230** treats the swipe control as **narrower** than the full 350 band.

### Change
1. **`constants/theme.ts`** — Added **`CHECKOUT_TRACK_WIDTH_PT = 304`** (width in pt on the **390** artboard), with a short comment pointing at **Figma 1:230**. Adjust this one number to match Dev Mode if your file says something else (e.g. 298–312).

2. **`components/CheckoutSlider.tsx`** — Track width is now  
   **`Math.min(CHECKOUT_TRACK_WIDTH_PT * (width / 390), width - 40)`**  
   so it scales with the screen like Discover/cart, stays under the safe horizontal margin, and is **~46pt narrower** than the old 350 cap at the baseline.

3. Cleaned up unused **`useState`** / **`setDragging`** / **`onBegin`** (they weren’t driving UI and were only producing lint noise).

The thumb drag math (`MAX_DRAG`, spring, “Done!” cross-fade) still uses the new `TRACK_WIDTH` automatically. If you paste the exact frame width from Figma for **1:230**, we can set `CHECKOUT_TRACK_WIDTH_PT` to that value precisely.

input.description: Verify TypeScript after cart StyleSheet fix

content.text:
Here’s what was wrong and what we changed for [Figma ~1:44](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-44&m=dev) (Discover header search) and [Figma ~1:207](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-207&m=dev) (cart gradient actions).

### Cause
iOS/Android draw **shadows on the view that owns them**. Putting **`overflow: 'hidden'`** on the **same** view as the gradient (for rounded corners) **clips the drop shadow** under the button, so the Figma-style **bottom glow** barely showed.

### Approach
Use a **two-layer shell** everywhere it mattered:

1. **Outer** — size, **`shadowColor` / `shadowOffset` (y > 0)** / `shadowRadius` / `elevation`, **`overflow: 'visible'`**, `position: 'relative'`.
2. **Inner** — **`overflow: 'hidden'`** + `borderRadius`, **`LinearGradient`** `absoluteFill` (and optional hairline border in a sibling with `pointerEvents="none"`).

### Files

**`app/index.tsx` (≈1:44 — header search)**  
- Search control: outer shell holds **#10141c** shadow (`y: lx.shadowUp`, `radius: lx.shadowBlur`, `elevation: 10`).  
- Inner clip + gradient + **`searchBtnIconLayer`** for the **hairline** + icon.

**`app/cart.tsx` (≈1:207 — back, Apply, +)**  
- **Back**: `backBtnShell` (shadow) → `backBtnClip` (gradient) → `backBtnHairline` → `backChevron`.  
- **Apply**: `applyBtnShell` → `applyBtnClip` → `applyBtnHit` → `applyBtnHairline`.  
- **Qty +**: `plusShell` (shadow) → `plusClip` (gradient) → `plusIconLayer` (hairline + icon).

**`components/CategoryPill.tsx` (category chips)**  
- Active, solid inactive, and frosted paths all use **shadow on the outer `pillSize` box** and **clip + fill inside**, so the **same bottom shadow** as Figma isn’t clipped.  
- Frosted **mask** again uses explicit **`sx.wIn` × `sx.hIn`** (replacing `flex: 1`) for reliable masking.

Shadow numbers stay aligned with what you already had in tokens (**`FIGMA_PRODUCT_CARD.shadowHex`**, **~20 / ~30** on Discover; cart **#10141c**, **y: 20**, **radius: 30** on back/Apply; slightly smaller shadow on the **24×24** `+` control).

<user_query>
how complex would it be to upgrade to expo54 
</user_query>

input.search_term: Expo SDK 54 release React Native version upgrade from SDK 52
input.explanation: Confirm Expo 54 pairing with RN and official upgrade expectations for an accurate complexity answer.

content.text:
For **this repo** (Expo **~52**, RN **0.76**, **expo-router** ~4, **Reanimated** ~3.16, **NativeWind** 4, **masked-view**, **expo-blur**, custom layouts), upgrading to **SDK 54** is **moderate complexity** if things go smoothly, and can turn **moderate–high** if you hit native or Reanimated issues.

### Why it’s not “trivial”
- **Two major jumps**: 52 → 54 usually means a **large RN bump** (SDK 54 aligns with **RN 0.81** per [Expo’s SDK 54 changelog](https://expo.dev/changelog/sdk-54) and the [upgrade guide](https://expo.dev/blog/expo-sdk-upgrade-guide)).
- **All `expo-*` packages** need to move in lockstep (`npx expo install --fix` after bumping `expo`).
- **Reanimated**: SDK 54’s story often involves **Reanimated 4**; your app leans on Reanimated (gestures, `CheckoutSlider`, tab bar, etc.). There is a **real migration surface** ([Reanimated 3 → 4 migration](https://docs.swmansion.com/react-native-reanimated/docs/guides/migration-from-3.x/)). Separately, **NativeWind** has historically been sensitive to Reanimated major versions—Expo’s upgrade notes have called out **staying on Reanimated 3 with NativeWind** in some cases; you’d need to confirm what **your** NativeWind + SDK 54 combo supports before committing.
- **Android**: SDK 54 pushes **edge-to-edge** defaults more strongly; apps with hand-tuned safe areas / bottom chrome (like your tab bar) sometimes need **layout follow-up** ([changelog](https://expo.dev/changelog/sdk-54)).
- **iOS**: **Precompiled React Native** (XCFrameworks) is mostly a **build-time** win/constraint, not app code—unless you rely on unusual Pod setup ([changelog](https://expo.dev/changelog/sdk-54)).

### Why it’s still “reasonable” for you
- You’re **Expo-managed** (no obvious heavy native modules beyond standard stack).
- Upgrade path is **well documented**: [Upgrade Expo SDK](https://docs.expo.dev/workflow/upgrading-expo-sdk-walkthrough/) + **SDK 54 changelog** + `expo-doctor`.

### Practical effort (order of magnitude)
- **Half day to a day** if: `expo upgrade` / bump `expo` → `expo install --fix` → fix a few types/deprecations → green `expo start` + iOS/Android smoke test.
- **Several days** if: Reanimated 4 migration touches many worklets/gestures, NativeWind forces a forked Reanimated version, or Android edge-to-edge needs rework on Discover/Cart chrome.

### Suggested approach
1. **Branch**, bump **`expo`** to 54, run **`npx expo install --fix`**, then **`npx expo-doctor`**.
2. Resolve **Reanimated + NativeWind** first (read current Expo 54 + NativeWind compatibility; decide **v3 vs v4** before rewriting gestures).
3. **Smoke-test** Discover / Detail / Cart + **physical Android** for insets.

So: **not a one-line change**, but **a normal Expo major upgrade**—complexity is mostly **dependency alignment + Reanimated + Android insets**, not your app logic itself.

Here’s what was going wrong and what we did.

**What you were seeing**  
The tab bar’s **outer** `View` was taller than the bar (`barH + safe area bottom`) and was filled with `TAB_BAR_GRADIENT.color1` — the **same** dark stop as in the gradient, but as a **flat** rectangle under the rounded SVG. **What you asked for**  
You didn’t want to **swap** that for another screen color (like `COLORS.bg`). You wanted that **extra painted band gone**, without changing the tab’s gradient.

**Change**  
Stop painting that layer: set the outer wrapper to **`transparent`**. The Figma gradient on the SVG is unchanged. The area under the home indicator is no longer a second fill — whatever is already behind the tab (your screen background) shows through.

The **active tab pill** was wrapped in a `View` with a heavy **iOS shadow** (`shadowColor: '#10141c'`, large positive `shadowOffset`, `shadowRadius`, plus **`elevation: 12` on Android**). That draws a soft dark plate **outside** the pill. With the tab sheet’s outer area transparent, that halo reads as a shadow sitting on the **app background** behind / around the bar.

**Change:** Drop all of those shadow props on that wrapper so the pill is drawn flat—gradient + stroke only—so nothing bleeds onto `COLORS.bg` behind the tab.

```254:268:c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome\components\BottomTabBar.tsx
          style={{

            left:     -PILL_PATH_X * scale,
            top:      -PILL_PATH_Y * scale,
            width:    PILL_SVG_W * scale,
            height:   PILL_SVG_H * scale,

        >
```

[REDACTED]

Here is what that strip is and how it ties to the bottom chrome (without being part of `BottomTabBar` itself).

## What you are seeing

It is almost certainly **not** a separate “shadow layer” inside `DiscoverBottomNav` / `BottomTabBar`. There is **no** semi-transparent scrim `View` in those components.

Two real effects stack together:

### 1. Decorative accent under the scroll (main “see‑through darkening”)

On Discover, the big cyan→indigo shape is an **`Svg` polygon** rendered **first**, with **`StyleSheet.absoluteFillObject`**, so it sits **under** the `ScrollView`:

```138:166:c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome\app\index.tsx
  return (
    <View style={[styles.safe, { paddingTop: insets.top }]}>
      {/* ─── Blue accent (1:40) — clipped so it never draws behind the tab bar wedge */}
      <Svg
        width={width}
        height={height}
        style={StyleSheet.absoluteFillObject}
        pointerEvents="none"
      >
        <Defs>
          <ClipPath id={bgAccentClipId}>
            <SvgRect x={0} y={0} width={width} height={bgAccentClipH} />
          </ClipPath>
          ...
        </Defs>
        <SvgPolygon
          clipPath={`url(#${bgAccentClipId})`}
          points={bgPolyPts}
          fill="url(#bgAccent)"
        />
      </Svg>

      {/* ─── Scrollable content ──────────────────────────────────────────────── */}
      <ScrollView
```

`ScrollView` has **no opaque background**, so anything that is not fully opaque can **composite with that polygon behind it**.

Most product tiles use **glass** fills (`fillOpacity` **0.6** on the card path in `CardBackground` for Rectangle 167 style), so you **see the accent through the card** — it reads as a **darker, slightly transparent veil** over the bike art.

As you scroll, **different parts** of that diagonal gradient sit behind each card, so the effect **moves** with the list.

### 2. Why it lines up with the nav (“attached”)

The accent is **clipped** with a height tied **explicitly** to the same numbers as the tab chrome:

```77:81:c:\Users\debob\Desktop\GymBros TakeHome\app\index.tsx
  const scale = width / 390;
  // Cyan accent: clip to the full bottom chrome height (Figma tab bar + device home inset).
  const tabBarVisualPx = TAB_BAR_FRAME_HEIGHT_PT * scale;
  const tabBarLayoutPx = tabBarVisualPx + insets.bottom;
  const bgAccentClipH = Math.max(0, height - tabBarLayoutPx);
```

So the accent polygon is cut off with a **horizontal** clip edge at **`height - tabBarLayoutPx`**, i.e. **aligned to the same bottom inset budget** as `BottomTabBar` (`TAB_BAR_FRAME_HEIGHT_PT` + `insets.bottom`). Here’s what was going wrong: **a transparent `View` still sits in the hit-testing order above the `ScrollView`**, so it was eating pans and taps over the tab region even with `backgroundColor: 'transparent'`.

**What we changed**

1. **Outer wrapper (line ~185)** — `pointerEvents="box-none"` so this view is not a target itself; only real targets inside get touches, and empty space (e.g. under the home inset) passes through.

2. **`barInner` (line ~199)** — `pointerEvents="box-none"` so gaps between the absolutely positioned layers aren’t claimed by an invisible full-size box.

3. **Tab sheet `Svg` (line ~202)** — `pointerEvents="none"` so the full-bleed SVG rectangle doesn’t steal touches on the slant / chrome; only the **icon `TouchableOpacity`s** stay interactive.

4. **Home indicator container** — `pointerEvents="none"` so that strip doesn’t block scrolling or taps.

Tab icons still use `TouchableOpacity` as before. The bar should look the same but **no longer block** interaction with the product grid when content scrolls under it.
